headerDisplay: Speak short phrases on your HomePod (or any AirPlay speaker). Each **button** below shows up in the Home app as a momentary switch — flip it on and the phrase is spoken, then it resets itself. Speech is synthesized fully offline with a bundled Piper voice; nothing is sent to the cloud.
footerDisplay: Playback uses the **homebridge-homepod-radio** `/play` route automatically when that plugin is installed; otherwise it streams directly with **pyatv** (`atvremote`). The optional LAN HTTP endpoint is **off by default** — only enable it on a trusted network.

name.title: Platform Name
name.description: Shown in the Homebridge log only.

phrase.title: Phrase to Speak
phrase.placeholder: Dinner is ready, come to the kitchen.

volume.title: Volume (0-100)
volume.description: Optional. Leave blank to use the default volume below.

homepodRadioPlayBase.title: homepod-radio /play Base URL
homepodRadioPlayBase.description: Used for the homepod-radio playback target.

mediaPath.title: Shared Media Directory
mediaPath.description: Directory the homepod-radio plugin reads WAV files from. The synthesized clip is written here before the /play route is triggered.

atvId.title: pyatv Device ID
atvId.description: Identifier of the target speaker for pyatv (from `atvremote scan`). Required for the pyatv playback target.

voice.title: Voice
voice.description: Piper voice key. Downloaded on install. Stick to a *low* tier voice on memory-constrained boards.

enableHttp.title: Enable LAN HTTP "speak anything" endpoint
enableHttp.description: When on, exposes POST /say (and GET /say?text=...) so other devices on your network can speak arbitrary text. No authentication — keep it on a trusted LAN only.

minAvailableMb.title: Minimum Free RAM (MiB) Before Synthesizing
minAvailableMb.description: Pre-flight memory gate. If MemAvailable is below this, the request is refused instead of risking an out-of-memory reboot on small boards. Set 0 to disable.

piperThreads.title: Piper Thread Cap
piperThreads.description: Optional. Fewer threads = lower CPU/memory spike, slower synthesis. Recommended: 1 on a single-core / low-RAM board.
